data.title: Salesforce Stock Boost: What's Really Happening?
data.description:
Salesforce Inc (NYSE:CRM) shares are trading higher on Thursday, lifted by a broad market rally. The S&P 500 is up 0.8% and the tech sector is gaining 0.9%, with major indices like the Nasdaq and Russell 2000 also in the green.

The move reflects stronger sentiment across growth and tech names, with Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary stocks also performing well, which are conditions that typically support companies like Salesforce.

Salesforce Technical Analysis

Despite today's bounce, the stock is still under pressure from a technical standpoint. Salesforce remains about 10.1% below its 20‑day simple moving average and 8.3% below its 100‑day, showing it has struggled to regain momentum. Shares are down 31.7% over the past year and sit much closer to their 52‑week lows than their highs.

Technical indicators also point to lingering weakness: the RSI sits at 26.01, signaling oversold conditions, and the MACD remains below its signal line, suggesting bearish momentum could persist in the near term.

The combination of oversold RSI and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum, indicating that while the stock is oversold, there is still bearish sentiment prevailing.

  • Key Resistance: $267.50
  • Key Support: $222.00

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions

The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $325.04. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Barclays: Overweight (Raises Target to $338.00) (Jan. 12)
  • RBC Capital: Sector Perform (Raises Target to $290.00) (Jan. 5)
  • BTIG: Initiated with Buy (Target $335.00) (Dec. 17, 2025)

Valuation Insight: While the stock trades at a premium P/E multiple, the strong consensus and 3% expected earnings decline suggest analysts view this growth as justification for the 43% upside to analyst targets.

Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for Salesforce, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Value: Risk (Score: 6.44) — Trading at a steep premium relative to peers.
  • Growth: Strong (Score: 66.68) — Indicates solid growth potential.
  • Quality: Strong (Score: 76.12) — Balance sheet remains healthy.
  • Momentum: Weak (Score: 7.98) — Stock is underperforming the broader market.

The Verdict: Salesforce’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a classic “High-Flyer” setup. While the Quality score indicates a healthy balance sheet, the low momentum score warns that the stock is currently underperforming, suggesting that investors should be cautious.

CRM Price Action: Salesforce shares were up 2.87% at $227.92 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: JackPress/Shutterstock

data.title: PayPal Bets On Agentic Commerce, Acquires Israel-Based Cymbio (CORRECTED)
data.description:
Editor’s Note: This article has been updated to clarify that Cymbio has supported PayPal's Store Sync service as a partner since October 2025.

PayPal Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:PYPL) announced Thursday that it has agreed to acquire Cymbio, tightening its focus on AI-driven commerce as shopping increasingly shifts toward conversational platforms.

The financial terms of the deal were not disclosed.

The acquisition brings in-house the infrastructure that has enabled merchants to place product catalogs directly within AI shopping environments since late 2025, a space where discovery and checkout are increasingly merging.

Cymbio is a multi-channel orchestration platform for merchants, enabling connectivity to a global network of marketplaces, retailers, social commerce channels and AI shopping experiences.

Founded in 2015 and based in Tel Aviv (Israel), Cymbio brings experience in marketplace integration and multi-channel commerce.

The transaction is expected to close in the first half of 2026, subject to customary conditions, reinforcing PayPal's push to stay relevant as AI reshapes how consumers shop.

Why It Matters?

The deal brings Cymbio's multi-channel orchestration technology into PayPal's agentic commerce stack, allowing sellers to surface products across tools such as Microsoft Copilot and Perplexity without changing their existing operations.

For PayPal, the objective is scale: keeping merchants visible as AI becomes a primary entry point for online shopping.

Michelle Gill, executive vice president and general manager of small business and financial services at PayPal, noted that while the companies have been partners since last year, the acquisition will further “enhance our agentic commerce capabilities and accelerate the expansion to more of our merchants.”

Cymbio’s systems have powered PayPal’s Store Sync service since its launch in October 2025. The platform distributes product data into AI interfaces and routes orders back to merchants’ current fulfillment and management systems. Brands including Abercrombie & Fitch, Fabletics, Newegg, and Adorama are already using the service.

The announcement comes as investor sentiment toward PayPal has improved amid broader trade-related developments. In a separate catalyst, PayPal shares rose after easing tariff concerns lifted confidence across global commerce and payments stocks.

PayPal held Cash and equivalents of $14.4 billion as of September 30, 2025.

PYPL Price Action: PayPal Holdings shares were up 0.93% at $56.41 during premarket trading on Thursday. The stock is near its 52-week low of $55.01, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: The $1M SUV Is Coming — And It Might Be An EV, Robb Report Says
data.description:
The seven-figure price tag for an SUV was once considered impossible, but now it appears to be inevitable, according to Robb Report. 

The shift isn't just about inflation; it's evidence that a million-dollar price tag creates its own value and prestige. 

As of early 2026, the ceiling for production SUVs is just under the half-million-dollar mark, according to Robb Report. Leading the pack is the Rolls-Royce Black Badge Cullinan, with a starting price of about $472,750. 

Don't Miss:

  • Missed Nvidia and Tesla? RAD Intel Could Be the Next AI Powerhouse — Just $0.85 a Share
  • Sam Altman Says AI Will Transform the Economy — This Platform Lets Investors Back Private Tech Early

Following closely are the Ferrari Purosangue, retailing at nearly $435,000, and the Bentley Bentayga Mulliner Extended Wheelbase at $340,000. 

SUVs Enter Hypercar Territory

Historically, the world's 40 most expensive cars have shared two traits: They have two doors and are classified as supercars or hypercars. 

SUVs have long been held back by their reputation as tools — vehicles meant for towing, off-roading or hauling families. 

However, the Super SUV era has changed that perception. Brands like Lamborghini, which was the Urus become its bestseller in 2017, and Aston Martin, with its 707-metric-horsepower DBX707, have proven that buyers will pay supercar prices for high-riding performance, Robb Report said. 

Trending: Blue-chip art has historically outpaced the S&P 500 since 1995, and fractional investing is now opening this institutional asset class to everyday investors.

The jump to $1 million is expected to be led by legacy manufacturers using two levers: custom craftsmanship and electric powertrains. 

McLaren is developing its first SUV, slated for release in 2028. Expected to be priced at more than $500,000, it will immediately join the upper echelon of the Super SUV class. 

EV Innovation Meets Bespoke Luxury

The first true $1 million SUV will likely be an all-electric, technologically groundbreaking model from a brand like Cadillac or Aston Martin, leveraging the same messaging Cadillac uses for its $360,000 Celestiq liftback sedan, according to Robb Report. 

History shows how explosive the luxury SUV's growth into the ultra-premium market has been. In 1987, the Land Rover Range Rover debuted in the U.S. at $30,000 — roughly $90,000 in today's inflation-adjusted dollars. At the time, Car and Driver questioned why anyone would pay luxury sedan prices for a glorified "dune buggy."

But the publication concluded that an expensive SUV may have its benefits. 

"Even at 70 mph over unpaved roads, it had a remarkably smooth ride, more like a Cadillac than a dirt donk," Car and Driver wrote.

Image: Shutterstock
